blockchain.definition: A shared digital record that stores information in linked blocks across many computers.
blockchain.analogy: Like a shared Google Doc that everyone can read but nobody can secretly edit.

block.definition: A container that holds a batch of transactions, like a page in a ledger.
block.analogy: Like a page in a notebook that gets sealed once it's full.

hash.definition: A unique digital fingerprint created from data that always produces the same result for the same input.
hash.analogy: Like a fingerprint — unique to each person and impossible to fake.

consensus.definition: The process by which all participants in a network agree on what's true.
consensus.analogy: Like a group vote where the majority must agree before anything is official.

distributed-ledger.definition: A record book that's copied across thousands of computers, with no single owner.
distributed-ledger.analogy: Like having identical copies of a town's record book in every house.

cryptocurrency.definition: Digital money that uses cryptography for security and runs on a blockchain without banks.
cryptocurrency.analogy: Like digital cash that doesn't need a bank to work.

token.definition: A digital asset that represents something of value and lives on an existing blockchain.
token.analogy: Like arcade tokens — they have value within their system.

bitcoin.definition: The first cryptocurrency, created in 2009 by the pseudonymous Satoshi Nakamoto.
bitcoin.analogy: Like digital gold — scarce, valuable, and not controlled by any government.

ethereum.definition: A blockchain platform that lets developers build applications and create tokens on top of it.
ethereum.analogy: Like an app store where anyone can build and run programs.

wallet.definition: A tool that stores the digital keys you need to access your cryptocurrency.
wallet.analogy: Like a keychain — it doesn't hold your money, but holds the keys to access it.

private-key.definition: A secret code that proves you own your crypto and lets you send it.
private-key.analogy: Like the PIN to your bank account — never share it with anyone.

public-key.definition: Your crypto address that others use to send you funds.
public-key.analogy: Like your email address — safe to share so others can reach you.

gas-fee.definition: The cost you pay to use a blockchain network, like a processing fee.
gas-fee.analogy: Like postage stamps — you need them to send anything through the network.

smart-contract.definition: A self-executing program stored on a blockchain that runs automatically when conditions are met.
smart-contract.analogy: Like a vending machine — put in the right amount and it automatically gives you what you selected.

nft.definition: A unique digital certificate of ownership stored on a blockchain.
nft.analogy: Like a signed certificate of authenticity for a piece of art.

defi.definition: Financial services (lending, borrowing, trading) that operate without banks or middlemen, using smart contracts instead.
defi.analogy: Like a farmer's market for money — people deal directly with each other.

mining.definition: The process of using computing power to validate transactions and add new blocks.
mining.analogy: Like solving a math puzzle — whoever solves it first gets to add the next page to the ledger and earns a reward.

staking.definition: Locking up your crypto to help validate transactions on the network and earn rewards.
staking.analogy: Like putting money in a savings account — you commit it for a period and earn interest.

decentralization.definition: A system where control is spread across many participants instead of being held by one authority.
decentralization.analogy: Like Wikipedia — thousands of editors instead of one publisher.

peer-to-peer.definition: A network where participants interact directly with each other without going through a central server.
peer-to-peer.analogy: Like passing a note directly to someone instead of sending it through a teacher.
